channel.title: Psychology Today – Latest
channel.description: Latest articles from Psychology Today (full content)

item.title: The Psychology of Likes: When Metrics Reshape Who You Are
item.description:
Social Media

The Psychology of Likes: When Metrics Reshape Who You Are

New book "Personal Branding for Introverts" explores the psychology of likes.

Posted March 2, 2026

Key points
  • Your brain treats every post like a slot machine and the unpredictability is exactly what hooks you.
  • Audience capture is subtle: small, rational adjustments compound until you barely recognize your content.
  • Substance outlasts virality. The "delicious burger" people return to beats the terrible one that trends.

You spend hours crafting a thoughtful post about something you genuinely care about. You hit publish, check back eagerly, and... crickets. But that off-the-cuff one-liner that popped into your head on the commute home? It's blowing up. The likes pour in, the comments multiply, and suddenly you're wondering: should I be posting more content like this?

This is a familiar scenario for many online creators, the beginning of what personal branding expert Goldie Chan calls "the quickest way to insanity online." In her new book, Personal Branding for Introverts, Chan is wary of this audience capture dynamic on social media: caring too much about the number of likes can fundamentally reshape not just what you create, but who you become. Understanding the psychological patterns at play can allow you to navigate the social media landscape with more balance and equanimity.

Your Brain on Social Media Metrics

When you pull a slot machine lever, you don't know if you'll win or lose. Your brain releases more dopamine in anticipation of an uncertain reward than it does for a guaranteed one. This is why checking your phone for notifications feels so compulsive. You're not just seeking the reward itself, you're hooked on the anticipation. This is the potency of variable reward schedules, and they’re a massive driver of behavior, both on social media, and on the slot machines in Las Vegas.

Social media metrics can quickly become a trap.
Source: mariia shalabaieva/Unsplash

Content creation also creates its own moments of anticipation, and a similar mode of variable, unexpected reward. Every time you post something online, you're essentially pulling that lever. Will this post get 10 likes? 100? 1,000? The unpredictability creates what psychologists call a "prediction error" in your brain's reward system. When engagement exceeds your expectations, your brain floods with dopamine. When it underperforms, you feel deflated. Either way, you're compelled to post again to resolve the uncertainty and drive new moments of anticipation.

This is why Chan describes metrics as "a Venus flytrap that, literally, you walk next to and it claps closed and you're stuck inside." You don't suddenly fall into an obsession with numbers. Instead, you gradually walk toward it, post by post. Before long, you can be trapped.

The real danger lies in what psychologists call the “over-justification effect,” when the extrinsic rewards (e.g., social media engagement metrics) outweigh the intrinsic ones (e.g., what you had originally wanted to express). Initially, you may create content because it's meaningful to you, because you have something to say. But as the metrics become more salient, your motivation shifts. You start creating for the numbers. Once that shift happens, you've fundamentally changed your relationship with your work.

article continues after advertisement

The Slow Drift of the Audience

The psychological literature on behavioral reinforcement helps explain why this process is so insidious. When certain behaviors (posting specific types of content) consistently produce rewards (likes, shares, comments), those behaviors get strengthened. The tricky part, though, is that you often don't notice it happening.

Audience capture is a slow and subtle phenomenon. Consider the economics concept of Keynes' beauty contest. In this thought experiment, you're asked to predict which faces will be voted most beautiful in a contest. The optimal strategy isn't to pick the faces you find most beautiful. It's to pick the faces you think others will find beautiful. Or more accurately, the faces you think others think others will find beautiful.

Content creation can easily fall into audience feedback loops.
Source: Nicholas Green/Unsplash

This same dynamic plays out every time you create content. You're not just expressing yourself, you're implicitly modeling what your audience wants to see. And because this process of building models of other minds (what psychologists call social cognition) happens automatically and unconsciously, you drift toward audience preferences without realizing it.

Chan has witnessed this firsthand. She notes that when creators focus on numbers, it often comes "to the detriment of the quality of the content that they put out." The content slowly morphs, becoming less about what the creator genuinely wants to express and more about what generates engagement. Your "brand core" begins to erode, replaced by an algorithmically optimized version of yourself.

This is what makes audience capture so psychologically powerful. It's not a dramatic transformation. It's a series of tiny, rational adjustments that compound over time until you look back and barely recognize what you've become.

The Quality Paradox in Content Creation

How, then, should one think about their online presence? Substance wins in the long run, even when metrics suggest otherwise.

article continues after advertisement
Goldie Chan has learned social media from the inside out, with over 800 consecutive daily videos on LinkedIn.
Source: daria nepriakhina/Unsplash

Chan uses a vivid analogy to explain this. "If you make an absolutely terrible burger, it goes viral," she says. "Very few people will come back to have that burger unless it's an ironic thing." But create a genuinely delicious burger? People return. They tell their friends. They become regulars.

The same principle applies to content creation. Viral moments driven by novelty or controversy rarely translate into sustained attention. But when you consistently create work with real substance, what Chan calls having "meat in your meal," you build something more valuable than a spike in engagement: you build a reputation.

Chan learned this through her own experience. Her first videos didn't explode. She remembers getting 10 likes on an early video and feeling genuinely thrilled: "These were 10 people that I didn't know, that had found my video, that had liked my video." That enthusiasm and curiosity, she argues, is what creators need to maintain, especially in the early days.

"Focusing too early on about the numbers, and not giving your... whatever you're making, time to breathe, and your personal brand time to breathe," Chan explains, "that is what it needs to grow."

The Path Forward

The metrics aren't going away, and neither is our brain's susceptibility to variable rewards. But by understanding these psychological mechanisms, we can make more conscious choices about what we create and why.

As Chan wisely notes, "the way to madness is to care about metrics that don't matter." The challenge is recognizing which metrics actually matter for your goals. If you're building a personal brand based on genuine expertise and authentic connection, then vanity metrics like likes are nearly meaningless. What matters is whether you're creating work that reflects your values, showcases your real thinking, and attracts the kind of attention that serves your long-term goals.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Matt Johnson Ph.D.

item.title: Education Leadership in Action
item.description:
Leadership

Education Leadership in Action

Kim Wallace’s new book helps school leaders to be game-changers.

Posted March 2, 2026

Key points
  • Decision fatigue and isolation are major pain points for those leading school systems.
  • Misalignment (e.g., between initiatives and professional learning) drains trust and effectiveness.
  • Professional learning must be personalized and job-embedded.
Source: Jaykumar Bherwani/Unsplash

The world is changing faster than ever before. Schools are having to change with it to prepare students for a future that will be even more different than today is from yesterday. Leaders of schools and school systems must steer their communities through a perfect storm of challenges, and many educators describe their days as nonstop triage: every decision feels high-stakes, time is scarce, and pressure comes from every direction.

In times like these, we look to our fellow experts for help. In January of 2022, when teachers and administrators were still reeling from the impact of the COVID pandemic, I interviewed educator Kim Wallace about how leaders can ensure that vital school initiatives succeed. Wallace formerly served as superintendent of one of the largest school districts in California, is associate director of the 21CSLA (California School Leadership Academy) State Center at the UC Berkeley School of Education, and consults through Process Makes Perfect. Wallace has a new book out that is just in time for the latest (as well as the old-but-persistent) challenges educators face today: Game-Changing Leadership in Action: An Educator's Companion. Wallace gives us a peek here at strategies that school leaders can use now.

Jenny Rankin (JR): What are some pain points that school and systems leaders are currently experiencing?

Kim Wallace (KW): School and systems leaders are navigating an unprecedented convergence of challenges. Many are operating in constant “crisis mode,” balancing staffing shortages, declining morale, mental health needs, and increasing accountability pressures—all while being asked to lead innovation and improvement.

A major pain point is decision fatigue. Leaders are making high-stakes decisions with limited time, incomplete information, and heightened scrutiny from multiple stakeholders. At the same time, many feel isolated—expected to be steady and confident while carrying the emotional weight of their organizations.

Another significant challenge is misalignment: initiatives that don’t connect to lived realities, professional learning that feels disconnected from daily work, and systems that prioritize compliance over coherence. These conditions can erode trust, energy, and long-term effectiveness.

JR: Tell me about your personalized professional learning approach to leadership.

KW: My motto is: leadership can be lonely, but it doesn’t have to be! My approach to professional learning is grounded in the belief that leadership growth must be personal, contextual, and practical. One-size-fits-all leadership development no longer works in complex educational systems.

Personalized professional learning begins with understanding who the leader is—their values, strengths, blind spots, and current realities. From there, learning is intentionally aligned to their role, their organizational context, and the challenges they are actually facing, not hypothetical ones.

article continues after advertisement

In my newest book, I emphasize learning that is job-embedded, reflective, and adaptive. Leaders engage in cycles of inquiry, apply strategies in real time, reflect on impact, and make adjustments. The goal isn’t just skill acquisition—it’s sustained growth that improves both leader capacity and system outcomes.

JR: How do the personal, environmental, and institutional domains factor into all this?

KW: Effective leadership operates at the intersection of three domains: personal, environmental, and institutional. The personal domain includes a leader’s mindset, identity, emotional intelligence, and well-being. Without self-awareness and internal alignment, even the best strategies fall short.

The environmental domain reflects the culture, relationships, and conditions in which leadership occurs. Trust, psychological safety, and collaboration are essential drivers of performance and innovation.

The institutional domain encompasses policies, structures, systems, and expectations. Sustainable change happens when institutional practices support the values and behaviors leaders are trying to model.

Game-changing leadership requires intentional alignment across all three. When leaders attend to only one domain, improvement is temporary. When they integrate all three, transformation becomes possible.

JR: Why (and how) should educational leaders think about long-term sustainability and success?

KW: Short-term wins are tempting—but unsustainable leadership models ultimately cost organizations people, momentum, and impact. Educational leaders must think long-term because schools and systems are human ecosystems, not machines. Sustainability means designing leadership practices that are resilient, distributive, and values-driven. It requires leaders to move from “doing more” to doing what matters most, building capacity in others rather than carrying everything themselves. In practice, this looks like prioritizing leader well-being, developing future leaders, establishing coherent systems, and making decisions through a long-range lens. Sustainable success is about creating conditions where leaders and organizations can adapt, learn, and thrive over time.

article continues after advertisement

JR: What are some examples of strategies that school and systems leaders can leverage to improve their leadership and impact?

KW: There are several high-leverage strategies leaders can use immediately:

  • Engage in structured reflection to examine decisions, assumptions, and impact—not just outcomes.
  • Build leadership capacity in others by distributing responsibility and intentionally mentoring emerging leaders.
  • Align initiatives to purpose, ensuring that strategies connect clearly to values, vision, and student needs.
  • Invest in relationships and trust, recognizing that culture drives results more powerfully than mandates.
  • Use data as a learning tool (not a compliance mechanism) to inform continuous improvement.

Ultimately, game-changing leadership is about intentional design. When leaders align who they are, how they lead, and the systems they shape, their impact becomes deeper, broader, and more enduring.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Jenny Grant Rankin PhD
